nativeRange.habitat: Outer lagoon and seaward reefs; commonly found sheltering in branching coral heads (notably Pocillopora eydouxi and Acropora colonies) at depths of about 1–50 m. Range extends from the Cocos-Keeling Islands east to Samoa and the Line Islands, north to southern Japan, and south to Lord Howe Island.

diet.notes: Accepts a varied diet of meaty foods (mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood), herbivore preparations, and quality flake or pellet foods. In the wild it has been documented feeding on zooplankton, copepods, and algae picked from the reef matrix around its host coral.

avoidWith: newly introduced damselfish in small tanks

summary: A small but pugnacious Indo-Pacific damselfish recognised by two dark vertical bars on a silvery-grey body and a blue-green tail; hardy and reef-safe but tends to become territorial as it matures.
careNotes: The Two Stripe Damselfish (Dascyllus reticulatus) is widely sold under several common names — Reticulate Dascyllus, Reticulated Damselfish, and Headband Humbug — and is closely related to other Dascyllus species in the trade (Four Stripe, Three Stripe, Domino/Threespot). It is generally considered an easy, hardy species and is often recommended to beginners, but its temperament shifts from manageable as a juvenile to notably aggressive as an adult; it has been known to harass similarly sized tankmates and to be territorial toward conspecifics, particularly new additions. For this reason it is best added late to a mixed community, or housed with other aggressive species. Sources disagree somewhat on temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 22–26°C (72–78°F) while FishBase records mean tropical sea temperatures of around 24.6–28.9°C for this species' habitat — the range used here (23–28°C) splits the difference and reflects what is reported across hobby and scientific sources. In the wild it typically shelters in branching corals, especially Pocillopora and Acropora, so aquascaping with similar structure tends to bring out its natural behaviour. It is generally considered reef-safe — it does not harm corals or motile invertebrates — though it may bully smaller tankmates out of food and territory.
breedingNotes: Oviparous demersal egg-layer. Males prepare a nest site by mouth-cleaning a patch of rock or dead coral, the female deposits adhesive eggs on the cleaned surface, and the male then guards and aerates the clutch until hatching. Captive breeding has been reported but is uncommon in home aquaria.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Source of retailer-style husbandry baseline: 30-gallon minimum, easy care, aggressive temperament, reef-safe, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, max 3.5 in.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacentridae), max size (9 cm TL), native range (Eastern Indian Ocean to Western Pacific, depth 1–50 m), habitat (outer lagoons and seaward reefs, associated with Pocillopora eydouxi), reproduction (oviparous, male nest cleaning, demersal eggs, paternal care), and habitat temperature range 24.6–28.9°C. FishBase records a warmer range than LiveAquaria; averaged to 23–28°C.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked common names (Reticulate dascyllus, two-stripe damselfish), max size (9 cm / 3.5 in), description (grey body with two vertical stripes and a blue-green tail), temperament (aggressive, harasses similarly sized fish), and aquarium hardiness.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked general damselfish husbandry parameters (temperature 73–81°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, omnivorous diet, reef-safe) used to corroborate LiveAquaria's species-specific figures.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous demersal egg-layer; males clean a nest site and guard adhesive eggs attached to rock or dead coral. Pair bonding occurs around the nest. Has been bred in captivity but rarely in typical home aquaria.
